articles.title: Seven Days to Fernie
articles.summary: My brother Samuel suggested we ride the BC Epic 1000.
articles.body:
![Sam and I on the big climb](/images/bc-epic/both-on-climb.JPG)

Samuel has a talent for suggesting things that sound unreasonable and somehow making them seem fun. The BC Epic 1000 — a self-supported bikepacking route from Merritt to Fernie, roughly a thousand kilometres through the mountains of southern BC — was one of those suggestions.

## The Setup

The BC Epic isn't really a race, at least not officially. No registration fees, no support crew, no prizes. Just a plotted route and a loose gathering of people who want to ride their bikes. We left a day before the official start so we could watch the actual racers depart.

I'd done a couple of overnighters before this. Samuel has been bikepacking for years and got me into it. My gear setup reflected the gap in our experience levels: a couple of poorly strapped dry bags that required constant stops to re-tighten. It held together. Mostly.

![Harry at the start of the offroading](/images/bc-epic/harry-start.JPEG)
![Samuel at the start of the offroading](/images/bc-epic/sam-start.JPEG)

## The Rail Grades

About 80% of the BC Epic follows old railway corridors — the Kettle Valley Railroad, the Columbia Western, the Great Northern. On paper, rail grades sound pleasant. Gentle gradients, no traffic, through beautiful country.

In practice, the gravel extends to the horizon and doesn't stop. You're riding down a tunnel of trees with no end in sight, just another kilometre ticking over on the GPS. By the end of a long rail grade day, your legs are toast and your head is exhausted.

The infrastructure along the old lines is still out there if you look for it. At one point I stopped at an outhouse — wooden, weathered, no door. Just open to the valley. I used it anyway. Best view I've had from that position. I have a photo.

Samuel's saddle sores showed up in the first couple of days and stayed for the rest of the trip. He still finished.

![Endless meadow track](/images/bc-epic/endless-meadow.JPG)

![Landslide scree](/images/bc-epic/landslide.JPG)

## The Race Leader

On day one we saw the guy who would win the whole thing. We were taking a dip in a lake when he came past — moving at a pace that didn't seem compatible with carrying enough gear to survive a week in the mountains. He wasn't carrying much gear. He finished in around two and a half days. We watched him disappear around a corner and then got back in the water.

![Sam's spooky swim](/images/bc-epic/spooky-swim.jpeg)

## The Good Parts

There was a pizza delivery to a campsite — I don't remember whose idea it was or how we arranged it, but it came with a couple of Bud Lights that we thought were a good idea until the next morning when they very clearly weren't.

There were railway tunnels, dark and cold, that you'd roll through and come out the other side blinking. There was a descent into one of the valley towns with a lake spread out below.

And there was our dad, who rode his bike from the other end of the route to meet us at the top of the big climb into Kimberley on the second-to-last day. He was just there, at the top, waiting. It was exactly what you need on day six of a thousand-kilometre bike ride.

![Sam entering a railway tunnel](/images/bc-epic/entering-tunnel.JPG)

![Sam in a gazebo overlooking a valley town](/images/bc-epic/gazebo.JPG)

## Fernie

We rolled into Fernie on day seven. There's not much to say about the finish that the gin and tonic didn't cover. It was cold and it was perfect and we sat there for a long time.

![Harry at Fernie City Hall](/images/bc-epic/harry-finish.JPEG)
![Samuel at Fernie City Hall](/images/bc-epic/sam-finish.JPEG)

Samuel is probably already planning the next one. Bolivia, I think.
